data.title: Global polymer market synopsis
data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, HDPE prices down adjusted in parts of South Asia while quoting stable in other parts of the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. In China, favourable macroeconomic conditions have persisted, resulting in an increasingly conducive market environment. Downstream manufacturers, motivated by the need to replenish stocks, have increased their acquisitions, especially at lower price levels. This has contributed to enhanced supply-demand conditions, with the polyethylene market remaining robust despite varying trends. In the U.S. dollar-denominated market, there has been a moderate rise in prices, with market quotes reflecting a rise from the previous period. The reduction in tariff tensions between China and the U.S. has stimulated terminal foreign trade orders, providing support for U.S. dollar prices. Nevertheless, the diminishing price differential between domestic and international markets has effectively closed the import window for most product categories. Forward-priced U.S. shipments experienced a slight increase, and overall market sentiment has improved compared to earlier periods. However, the actual effects will hinge on customs declaration results following May 13. Looking forward, the broader market outlook remains weak in spite of short-term optimism, with the U.S. dollar market likely to continue exhibiting signs of weakness. In Southeast Asia, the polyethylene market has shown stability, bolstered by positive macroeconomic developments that have enhanced market sentiment. The introduction of favourable tariff policies has significantly increased confidence among traders and investors. This optimistic outlook has prompted traders to strategically reduce their inventory levels, aiming to take advantage of the favourable conditions or to manage their stock effectively. The market has experienced a positive trend due to these encouraging macroeconomic factors, while lower import offers have been retracted. Last week, several major suppliers from the Middle East have nearly depleted their current allocations and are now anticipating the release of new quotas for June shipments. These new quotas are expected to be announced in the last week of May, potentially impacting market dynamics upon their release. The current stability of the market is primarily attributed to supply limitations resulting from the temporary withdrawal of lower offers and the forthcoming availability of new import options. Nevertheless, trading activity is projected to increase in the latter part of May as new offers for June-loading cargoes start to surface. In India, the domestic polyethylene (PE) market continues to exhibit a generally weak sentiment, primarily due to a lack of robust buying interest. PE grades, except LDPE grades, have remained relatively stable owing to limited market availability. Most purchasers have chosen to restrict their acquisitions to immediate needs, primarily sourcing from local manufacturers. This cautious strategy is a reflection of the prevailing pessimistic market outlook. Domestic suppliers have made efforts to boost demand by providing price incentives; however, anticipated changes in trade flows are likely to limit short-term import demand. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the majority of buyers opted to remain inactive, preferring a passive role instead of engaging in the market. This prudent position is largely attributed to their wait-and-see strategy, as they are anticipating new import offers from overseas suppliers. LDPE: This week, Low density polyethylene prices journeyed southward in the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. In China, the LDPE market is currently characterized by weak demand, as purchasers are either engaging in minimal buying or restricting themselves to inquiries. The overall market sentiment is subdued, influenced in part by the slow recovery of demand and ongoing geopolitical and trade tensions between the United States and China, leading many buyers to take a cautious, wait-and-see stance. In Vietnam, the LDPE market continues to exhibit weak trends. Nevertheless, import offers to Vietnam and Malaysia have remained stable for the second consecutive week, leading to speculation that the recent downward trend may be approaching a cyclical low. However, buyers are still placing bids at lower levels, and a few transactions completed at these prices have further intensified the downward pressure on the market. In India, market sentiment has remained distinctly pessimistic as buyers have largely halted their purchases in response to a significant price decline. To boost demand, domestic producers have introduced various pre- and post-sales discount schemes. Despite this, buyers have remained cautious, delaying purchases in expectation of additional price corrections. Local suppliers have also implemented price protection strategies to bolster order flows, yet overall demand has remained tepid. Processors are operating with limited stockpiles and adopting a wait-and-see approach, influenced by ongoing price volatility. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the majority of purchasers have preferred to remain on the sidelines, exercising caution while they await new import offers from international suppliers. This cautious stance is indicative of a wider wait-and-see approach in light of the ongoing market volatility. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices slipped in Asia. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. In China, prices of LLDPE have decreased, mainly due to reduced offers from international suppliers, even though favourable macroeconomic conditions have persisted, contributing to a more positive market environment. Downstream manufacturers, driven by the need to replenish stocks, have increased their purchases, especially at lower price levels. This has strengthened the supply-demand balance, keeping the polyethylene market robust despite varying trends. In the U.S. dollar market, prices have seen a partial rise, with market quotes increasing relative to the previous period. The easing of tariff tensions between China and the U.S. has enhanced foreign trade orders, thereby supporting U.S. dollar prices. Nevertheless, the diminishing price disparity between domestic and global markets has effectively closed the import opportunities for most product categories. Today, forward-priced U.S. shipments have seen a minor increase, and overall market sentiment has improved compared to earlier periods. However, the true effect will hinge on the outcomes of customs declarations after May 13. Despite short-term optimism, the broader market outlook remains weak, and the U.S. dollar market may continue to show signs of weakness. In Southeast Asia's Linear Low Density Polyethylene (LLDPE) import market, prices have decreased while market activity has remained low, characterized by limited offers and transactions. Overall demand is weak, as numerous plastic converters expect a decline in orders for finished products, especially from U.S. clients due to ongoing trade tensions and tariffs. Most major suppliers from the Middle East have exhausted their current allocations and are now anticipating new quotas for June shipments, which are expected to be announced in the last week of May. In Vietnam, purchasing sentiment is sluggish, influenced by the ongoing availability of competitively priced U.S.-origin cargoes, some of which have been redirected from China. In Indonesia, a significant price reduction by a key regional producer has further weakened sentiment, leading buyers to lower their bids and postpone purchases. Nevertheless, trading activity may increase in the latter half of May as new offers for June-loading cargoes start to surface. In India, the LLDPE market has generally exhibited weakness, primarily due to a lack of buying interest. Prices for LLDPE have fallen to a three-month low, while other polyethylene grades have maintained relative stability owing to limited market supply. Most purchasers have restricted their acquisitions to immediate needs, predominantly sourcing from local manufacturers. This cautious approach stems from a generally pessimistic market outlook. Domestic suppliers have sought to boost demand through price incentives; however, anticipated changes in trade flows are likely to limit short-term import demand. Additionally, escalating geopolitical tensions contribute to market uncertainty, potentially affecting regional shipping operations and increasing freight costs. Domestic buying activity has remained subdued, with buyers awaiting the announcement of new Memorandums of Understanding (MOUs) and schemes from Indian producers for the new financial year. Market participants anticipate an increase in spot buying by mid to late April, following the release of MOUs and the signing of new contracts. Sentiment in the market has been cautious as stakeholders await government funding announcements and tender releases for infrastructure projects, which are deemed essential for enhancing demand for compound grades in April and May, prior to the monsoon season that typically hampers construction activities nationwide. Indian producers have already introduced discount schemes for May 2025. Nevertheless, weak demand from end-users and sufficient product availability in the domestic market have led to a cautious market perspective. Consequently, most traders have adopted a wait-and-see strategy amid ongoing uncertainty and price volatility. Even though demand remains tepid, the Indian polyethylene market remains adequately supplied, with no adverse impact foreseen on material avails in spite of two scheduled plant turnarounds this month. Given the ample availability, buyers are unlikely to encounter any supply-side pressures. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the market undertone was largely muted with hardly any trading activity witnessed as participants awaited fresh offers. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices down adjusted in the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. Chinese cargoes have been withdrawn from the market, and buyers are now awaiting fresh July shipment offers. Recent macroeconomic trends have created a more optimistic market atmosphere. The introduction of favourable tariff policies has notably boosted confidence among traders and investors, leading to a more optimistic outlook. Consequently, buyers are currently refraining from making purchases as they monitor the evolving market conditions and await new offers from international suppliers. Prices marched higher in Far east Asia and Southeast Asia following the recent reduction in tensions between China and the United States which has enhanced overall sentiment within the commodity markets. The rise in crude oil prices has further bolstered confidence throughout the petrochemical sector. On the supply front, the polypropylene (PP) market is still facing tight conditions due to ongoing maintenance at production facilities. The decrease in production levels has alleviated inventory pressures, providing fundamental support for prices. However, demand is currently weak as the market enters a seasonal slowdown. Limited orders from downstream sectors and a lack of enthusiasm for procuring raw materials have restricted the potential for substantial price hikes. Nevertheless, encouraging macroeconomic news�especially the introduction of favorable tariff regulations�has uplifted market sentiment. Traders are actively selling off their inventories, although downstream purchasers are primarily concentrating on meeting only their immediate needs. Raw material consumption remains inadequate, prompting upstream manufacturers to persist in reducing their stock levels. Interest in replenishment from downstream buyers is limited, and the growth of orders is sluggish. Nonetheless, ongoing supply constraints due to significant maintenance-related losses continue to provide price support, indicating that the polypropylene market is likely to remain stable to firm in the near future. In India, the prices of polypropylene (PP) have stabilized following a decline over the previous five weeks, attributed to limited demand for imported materials. Consequently, market discussions have been minimal. Processors have primarily focused on purchasing based on necessity, preferring domestic sources and maintaining a cautious approach in light of the prevailing bearish market conditions. The price incentives provided by local suppliers have enhanced the appeal of domestic materials. Looking forward, a decline in downstream activities across significant sectors is anticipated to negatively affect PP demand in the upcoming weeks. Sectors such as raffia bags utilized in construction and agriculture, as well as rigid packaging applications including paints, ice creams, and beverages, are expected to see a decrease in PP demand. Additionally, the rising geopolitical tensions in South Asian region are being closely monitored by participants, as they may disrupt shipping operations and lead to higher freight costs. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, the majority of purchasers opted to remain on the sidelines, taking a prudent stance as they anticipate new import offers from overseas suppliers. This cautious approacj signifies a calculated strategy in light of prevailing market ambiguities. PS: Polystyrene prices continued to be steady in Asia this week. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. China's import demand has remained consistent, as local shipments are still offered at lower prices compared to imports from other Asian countries. Conversely, there has been a notable increase in competitive PS offers from China in various regions, including Southeast and South Asia, as Chinese exporters aim to clear excess inventory and broaden their sales activities in international markets. The fundamentals of the Southeast Asian spot PS market have remained mostly stable compared to the prior week, with an ongoing oversupply situation. Certain market participants observed a slight uptick in spot demand relative to the previous week, potentially influenced by early-month procurement activities. However, the demand for general purpose polystyrene (GPPS) in Southeast Asia has remained relatively consistent, bolstered by its various downstream applications. ABS: ABS prices quoted lower in South Asia, while remaining stable in Far east Asia and Southeast Asia this week. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. The Asian ABS market exhibited stability in both China and Southeast Asia, with prices in China reflecting tradable indications while Southeast Asia maintained a slight premium over China. Market sentiment remained cautious due to elevated inventories in China and the commencement of new production units, which contributed to the existing capacity. A producer indicated that although upstream styrene monomer prices are on the rise, demand is anticipated to remain weak in the short term. This is primarily because numerous downstream buyers depend on exports, and the ongoing uncertainties surrounding international tariff negotiations are causing them to postpone their purchases. Upstream markets displayed a general upward trend, with notable gains observed in key feedstocks. In the South Asian region, ABS prices have decreased despite the rising values of upstream styrene, butadiene, and ACN. The current market dynamics indicate a price decline primarily driven by reduced import offers, which have negatively impacted overall price levels. However, these lower offers are now being retracted as suppliers move away from earlier low-price strategies. Recent macroeconomic trends have created a more optimistic market atmosphere. Importantly, the introduction of beneficial tariff policies has greatly improved confidence among traders and investors, leading to a more favorable outlook. PVC: This week, PVC prices quoted flat across the Asian region. The temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. The spot market for polyvinyl chloride (PVC) in Asia witnessed a decline in trading activity. This decelerated momentum was mainly attributed to the fact that most buyers had already restocked their inventories for May, resulting in a diminished urgency to acquire further supplies. Consequently, trading volumes remained low. During this time, market participants redirected their attention to potential factors that could affect PVC negotiations and pricing for the upcoming June shipments. Overall, the market's cautious stance indicates a temporary halt in immediate trading, with focus shifting to elements that may influence future market dynamics. Additionally, some market participants observed that the anticipated monsoon seasonal slowdown in India and certain Southeast Asian nations in Q3 could further affect demand. In China, PVC prices exhibited a notable degree of stability. Domestic prices remained confined within a limited range, bolstered by continuous restocking efforts following the holiday season. This replenishment activity post-holiday contributed to the maintenance of price stability, even in the face of market activity fluctuations. Concurrently, export prices for PVC from the region also remained consistent, despite a downturn in export demand. The consistent export pricing indicates that suppliers chose to uphold their price levels in light of diminished overseas demand, potentially to preserve market share or in anticipation of a future recovery in demand. In summary, the market experienced a phase of price stability across both domestic and export sectors amidst varying supply-demand conditions. In India, trade sources have reported that discussions regarding exports have been muted, as buyers are taking a cautious wait-and-see approach. This reluctance stems from the current market volatility and the growing uncertainty related to forthcoming regulatory changes in India. In particular, there are rising concerns about the anticipated enforcement of PVC import restrictions in India, which may include antidumping duties and regulations imposed by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS). These potential trade obstacles are prompting buyers to refrain from making commitments, resulting in limited negotiations and a careful market atmosphere as stakeholders brace for the upcoming regulatory changes. The seller noted that various factors are currently impeding PVC exports from India. They specifically highlighted that the imminent monsoon season poses logistical and operational challenges, likely due to weather-related disruptions that could impact transportation and manufacturing processes. Moreover, they pointed out that antidumping duties designed to shield domestic industries from unfairly priced imports are creating financial barriers, rendering Indian PVC less competitive in global markets. Additionally, the controls enforced by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS), which entail strict quality and safety regulations, introduce regulatory challenges that exporters must address, potentially leading to increased compliance costs and delays. Together, these elements�seasonal weather challenges, trade protection measures, and regulatory requirements�are currently hindering the growth and facilitation of PVC exports from India. PET: This week, PET prices experienced sharp gains in the Asian region. The rise in PET prices within the region was influenced by a variety of interrelated factors. Firstly, increased upstream energy prices led to elevated production costs throughout the supply chain. Furthermore, the prices of essential feedstocks such as MEG, PTA, and PX experienced significant increases. Given that these feedstocks are critical raw materials for PET production, their heightened costs directly affected the overall manufacturing expenses. As the prices of MEG, PTA, and PX rose, producers encountered increased input costs, which they subsequently passed on to the market. In order to maintain their profit margins and avert financial losses, producers are necessitated to pass these elevated costs onto end consumers by increasing the selling prices of PET. This price modification is crucial for safeguarding profit margins in light of the rising raw material costs. The downstream polyester yarn and fiber market has also continued to rise, propelled by the increasing costs of upstream feedstocks. Nevertheless, there has been minimal discourse or activity in the regional market concerning these changes. Meanwhile, the temporary 90-day suspension of tariffs, agreed upon by the United States and China, has sparked renewed optimism that the prolonged trade dispute between the world's two largest economies might soon be resolved. This development has significantly reduced investor concerns about the potential for a global economic slowdown and has also led to increased expectations for a rise in global oil demand. The previous day's offer for PET bottle-grade, from a Chinese producer, was pegged at the USD 790�795/mt on FOB basis. Following discussions early morning, the producer officially increased its offer to around USD 820-825/mt on FOB basis. This marks an increase of approximately USD 30/mt from the previously stated range. Despite this upward adjustment, there have been no signs or reports of negotiations or discussions taking place at the new offer price level. Polyethylene Terephthalate bottle grade is experiencing a favourable trend within the polyester industry, propelled by favourable macroeconomic policies and seasonal demand. The increase in upstream raw material costs, heightened operating rates, and a rise in both domestic and international orders suggest that China's PET sector is exhibiting resilience and stability in its supply chain. The People's Bank of China implemented interest rate and reserve requirement reductions in early May that surpassed market expectations, thereby providing robust support to the real economy and capital markets. Concurrently, a positive advancement in China-U.S. trade relations has further revitalized export-oriented industries. In South East Asia, the Ministry of Investment, Trade and Industry (MITI) of Malaysia formally declared the implementation of definitive anti-dumping duties on polyethylene terephthalate (PET) resin imports from China and Indonesia on May 7, 2025. The announcement stated that the anti-dumping investigation, which commenced on August 9, 2024, found that certain PET products were being imported into the Malaysian market at prices lower than those in their respective domestic markets, causing significant harm to Malaysia's local PET industry. Based on the conclusive findings, duty rates varying from 2.29% to 11.74% have been implemented on imports from China while a fixed duty rate of 37.44% has been implemented on imports from Malaysia. These duties will be enforced by the Royal Malaysian Customs Department for a duration of five years, from May 7, 2025, to May 6, 2030. Europe HDPE: This week, HDPE prices trended lower in the European region. The current downward pricing pressure on the European high-density polyethylene (HDPE) market can be attributed to competitive material offers from the United States . According to recent market reports, prices have rapidly dropped over the last week. With a large influx of import offers from both the US and Turkey, injection grades of HDPE have been the most negatively impacted of the different grades. While there have been isolated cases of supply shortages due to delays in some anticipated shipments, these disruptions have had little effect on the dynamics of the market as a whole. Due to a rise in market offers, the price premium between HDPE blow molding and injection grades has also decreased. Participants in the market have frequently described demand as being weak, especially in the spot market where there is a clear lack of desire to purchase more volumes. Demand has been steady in the packaging industry, but it is still low in other derivative segments, particularly in the construction and automotive industries. Contractual negotiations have been ongoing for the past week. According to reports, some converters have asked for price reductions that are more significant than those brought about by the drop in monomer prices. The general mood of the market suggests that more price reductions are desired, especially from companies that did not offer reductions in April and are now facing an influx of material. Consequently, these producers are becoming more and more willing to negotiate. Reductions equal to the complete monomer decrease have been provided by certain domestic producers. However, with most market participants opting to postpone settlements until the end of the month in the hopes of better terms, few finalized contract prices have been reported. The European polyethylene (PE) market is expected to face continuous challenges, with demand expected to remain muted, in addition to immediate restocking requirements. This prediction is based on a stagnant economy where the risks of recession run high. With the exception of a few applications related to fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) and the comparatively robust flexible packaging industry, most buyers are expected to limit their purchases to necessary quantities and avoid making speculative purchases. Although it is too soon to tell, a backwardated pricing structure for polyethylene might result from a large increase in upstream crude oil production and the ensuing effect on feedstock prices. In the event that this happens, buyers might decide to further reduce their current stockpiles with the goal of restocking at a more advantageous price in the upcoming months. There is still uncertainty about whether the market has levelled off at a lower threshold, indicating the formation of a new equilibrium, or whether the modest recovery is just the result of front-loading during the 90-day EU-US reciprocal tariff suspension. In the UK, where companies are stepping up cost-cutting efforts in the face of worsening manufacturing conditions, the outlook is still especially dire. The situation is being made worse by weak domestic consumer confidence and dwindling export demand, which is forcing businesses to cut back on production. Spot prices for all grades of polyethylene have generally decreased throughout the week. Although some sellers have not yet provided final offers, several have already enacted substantial price reductions on medium- to large-volume orders set for delivery in May. These decreases, which reflect considerable discounts relative to previous spot prices in May, highlight the varied sentiments among sellers. Nevertheless, additional confirmation of these price trends is required, as the market looks for greater clarity in the upcoming weeks. LDPE: This week, LDPE prices journeyed southward in the European region. The European low-density polyethylene (LDPE) market has continued to experience a decline in prices over the past week, primarily influenced by competitive pricing from the United States. Reports suggest that prices are falling swiftly, as the influx of imported materials puts additional strain on domestic markets. The LDPE sector is particularly affected, facing the most considerable price reductions due to robust import offers from both the US and Turkey, which heighten competition. Although some expected shipments have faced delays, leading to localized supply constraints, these interruptions have had a negligible effect on the overall market dynamics. Additionally, the price differential between various polyethylene grades has diminished as a result of an increase in market offers, further exacerbating competitive pressures. Demand continues to be characterized as weak, especially in the spot market, where the desire for additional volumes is notably low. In the derivatives sector, packaging demand has remained relatively stable; however, consumption in the automotive and construction industries is still sluggish, indicative of broader economic uncertainties. Ongoing contractual negotiations have seen some converters seeking greater reductions than those corresponding with the decline in monomer prices. Overall, market sentiment indicates that those who previously avoided price reductions in April are now more willing to engage in negotiations due to increasing material availability. While certain domestic producers have implemented full reductions in accordance with the monomer price drop, few finalized contract prices have been reported. Many participants in the market are delaying deal closures, opting to wait until later in the month in hopes of achieving more advantageous pricing as the supply situation continues to change. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices slipped sharply in the European region. This week, the European linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE) market faced renewed downward pressure due to weak demand and a rise in imports from the United States. The situation was exacerbated by a ¬70/tonne reduction in the feedstock ethylene monthly contract price (MCP) for May, which further impacted LLDPE spot prices. Nevertheless, a clearer perspective on contractual pricing has yet to emerge, as numerous negotiations are still in progress or have not yet started. There was notable aggressive selling activity across all LLDPE grades, particularly from distributors looking to reduce excess inventories. This influx of discounted offers is occurring despite May generally being a seasonal restocking period for various downstream sectors in anticipation of the upcoming summer slowdown. Sellers have lowered their prices for immediate volumes as a result of increased pressure brought on by the anticipated arrival of competitively priced polyethylene imports from the US in June. In order to prevent possible inventory devaluation, some market participants are rushing to sell off their current stocks because it is anticipated that the replacement costs for these incoming shipments will be much lower. The need to clear excess volumes became more pressing as the week went on, especially for sellers whose stock levels were higher than anticipated. Because sellers now appear more inclined to make compromises in order to close deals, buyers now have more negotiating power in ongoing discussions. There are, nevertheless, some divergent viewpoints in the market. Higher stock level sellers are taking a more aggressive pricing strategy, while some sellers with comparatively balanced inventories are less pressured to provide significant discounts. As the market tries to find equilibrium in the face of shifting supply-demand dynamics, this discrepancy is creating a mixed pricing environment with the potential for additional volatility. PP: This week, PP prices plunged in the European region. The European polypropylene market experienced additional price declines over the week, influenced by sluggish demand and reduced feedstock costs. Concurrently, spot prices plummeted to their lowest point since January 2024, as a surge in import offers led to lower offers from sellers. This situation, coupled with a Euro 65/mt reduction in the May monthly contract price (MCP) for feedstock propylene, has applied downward pressure on PP spot prices. The significant declines may indicate some concern among sellers amid persistently low demand, potentially motivating them to mitigate the risks associated with excess supply on their balance sheets. Demand for spot material remained weak, primarily due to ongoing low demand from key automotive and construction sectors. Although packaging demand held steady, it was not enough to counterbalance the rising supply in the market. Market participants indicated that an increase in demand is unlikely under the current economic conditions. Additionally, uncertainty regarding the implications of US-China tariffs and trade negotiations has led buyers to postpone material purchases. On the supply front, the availability of PP remained robust, exacerbated by subdued activity in the spot market. Competitive offers for materials from the Middle East and Asia further enhanced PP availability. However, logistical challenges are causing delays for both local suppliers and importers. While such conditions might have supported pricing in different circumstances, the current fundamentals, particularly weak demand, are preventing any stabilization of prices. The decline in spot prices occurred despite several plant shutdowns. Normal supply has yet to be restored from polypropylene plants in Spain and Portugal that ceased operations following a sudden power outage on April 28. Although some of these facilities are slowly coming back online, their operational status remains uncertain. Additionally, a cracker in northern France is still offline and has declared force majeure regarding the supply of feedstock propylene. However, the nearby polypropylene plant has resumed production by sourcing propylene through imports. Furthermore, a polypropylene plant in the Netherlands is set to begin a scheduled turnaround in the last week of May, which is expected to last until the end of June. Market participants noted minimal differences in the performance of copolymer and homopolymer injection-grade materials, although the price gap between the two grades widened due to residual localized demand for copolymer. Contract pricing has also experienced downward pressure due to the reduction in the May propylene monthly contract price. The market aims to transfer the complete decline in the monomer price to polypropylene producers, who are attempting to maintain their pricing position; however, the healthy supply complicates their efforts. The realization of such pricing targets remains uncertain, particularly if weak demand persists in the upcoming weeks. Conversely, buyers are focused on ensuring that the full decrease in monomer prices is reflected in their polypropylene contracts. Some buyers anticipate that polypropylene contract prices may face additional pressure later in May, especially if there is an oversupply in the market. Despite the decline in spot prices increasing the disparity with contract prices, producers are of the opinion that it is essential for polypropylene (PP) contract margins to remain stable. They highlight the challenges posed by low margins and a demand landscape that jeopardizes the financial viability of their operations in Europe, which are already at a competitive disadvantage compared to other regions. This situation is underscored by a series of asset evaluations and recent announcements of permanent closures. The most recent development occurred, with a producer reporting a halt in the refurbishment of its Wilton cracker in the UK, attributing the decision to economic uncertainty and difficult market conditions Concurrently, trade tensions between the US and EU present an additional layer of uncertainty for market stakeholders. On May 8, the European Commission initiated a public consultation regarding a list of US imports, alongside those already designated for tariff implementation, which may face EU countermeasures if the ongoing negotiations do not yield a favourable resolution and the US tariffs are not lifted. Polypropylene imports from the US were included in this consultation list. The EU does not rely heavily on US-sourced PP imports, as the price differentials often render this trade unviable. The consultation period will remain open until June 10, after which a final proposal for countermeasures will be drafted, along with a legal framework for their enforcement should negotiations with the US fail to achieve satisfactory outcomes. This development can be interpreted in various ways; however, it may be contended that the announcement itself does not indicate any significant progress between the two parties thus far. Nevertheless, should negotiations lead to a breakthrough, particularly concerning automotive exports, it could enhance PP demand in Europe from that sector, which has been adversely affected by low demand and challenging market conditions. PS: This week, PS contract prices drifted lower, while spot prices remaining flat in the European region. The contract values for polystyrene have experienced a week-over-week decline, mirroring the decrease in the European styrene May contract price, which has settled at Euro 40/mt. Despite the reduction in polystyrene prices, imports are reported to remain competitively priced, with a distributor sourcing North African material for the production of extruded polystyrene, commonly used for insulation in the construction sector. Demand for May is expected to remain stable overall, similar to April, with no signs of significant fluctuations. However, minor variations are noted among downstream sectors, particularly influenced by seasonal factors from the construction industry in spring. Meanwhile, demand in packaging remains steady, while automotive demand is weak. Market participants anticipate that polystyrene demand is likely to remain flat and stable for the remainder of 2025. Downstream sectors continue to be influenced by macroeconomic factors, including inflation and interest rates, with recent inflation rates remaining steady and elevated despite some reductions observed thus far in 2025. Supply remains balanced to long, as producers adjust outputs in line with demand. Uncertainty lingers regarding the potential impacts of US tariffs on the supply chain, with market participants awaiting further information. Additionally, upstream styrene production faces challenges, as a styrene facility in Gonfreville is undergoing maintenance, and there is also maintenance at the joint venture Ellba. Furthermore, a force majeure has been declared at the propylene oxide/styrene monomer (POSM) plant in Tarragona, along with the permanent closure of a plant in Maasvlakte, the Netherlands, scheduled for October 2025. The effects of these upstream production issues on downstream polystyrene remain uncertain, and industry players are closely tracking the situation. Alongside competitive pricing, the steady reduction in container freight rates has opened up arbitrage opportunities between Asia and Europe. Nevertheless, in spite of these advantageous pricing conditions and lower freight rates, imports continue to face delays, requiring buyers to place orders for necessary quantities two months in advance. These delays are linked to the rerouting of vessels around the Cape of Good Hope, a result of Houthi rebel attacks on merchant ships in the Red Sea since December 2023. Previously, vessels would typically transit through the Suez Canal in the Red Sea. Additionally, the delays in deliveries have also impacted the transport of materials from the Mediterranean Sea to Europe. The market for expanded polystyrene (EPS) contracts experienced a decrease in prices on a month-to-month basis; however, the reductions were not as pronounced as those observed in GPPS. This decline in prices was linked to fluctuations in the styrene spot market, which plays a crucial role in determining EPS pricing, as nearly 60% of EPS production in Europe depends on purchases of spot styrene cargoes. ABS: This week, ABS contract prices nosedived, while spot prices were left unchanged in the European region. In Europe, the prices of ABS have shown a declining trend, influenced by a notable drop in upstream butadiene prices and a decrease of Euro 40/mt in the May contract price for European styrene. This decline was further exacerbated by a bearish market climate. At the same time, high production levels in the region led to competitive market offers. The appliance and automotive sectors, which are significant consumers of ABS, saw only a slight recovery, while the UK's industrial production continued to decline. Although some companies have implemented production cuts and facility shutdowns, supply has consistently outstripped demand, resulting in reduced margins and profitability. The high energy costs in Europe, along with structural inefficiencies, have created additional obstacles for production. Despite initial expectations for a modest increase in manufacturing indices, demand from the automotive and appliance industries in Europe has not shown substantial improvement. Supply remains strong, and distributors in the European ABS market maintain a largely pessimistic view of market demand. The oversupply situation has prompted aggressive market offers and heightened competition, while local production levels have remained high despite weak demand fundamentals. The European acrylonitrile butadiene styrene market has shown stability over the week; however, a slight uptick in demand is expected due to the 90-day tariff suspension between the United States and China. Currently, there are no reports of imports entering the European ABS market, as participants remain cautious about potential retroactive tariffs related to an ongoing anti-dumping duty investigation. While there are anticipations for seasonal demand growth in the construction industry, this rise is expected to have a negligible effect on the ABS market due to the generally low demand. Furthermore, other vital sectors, such as the automotive industry, are still facing weak demand without indications of recovery. Consequently, the global ABS market is expected to remain strained in the near term, as supply continues to surpass demand, and with ongoing structural issues in Europe and sluggish demand in Asia, a substantial price recovery seems unlikely. PVC: This week, PVC prices rolled over in the European region. The European polyvinyl chloride market experienced stable pricing, attributed to recent market activity following the announcement of a new monthly contract price for the feedstock ethylene, which decreased by Euro 70/mt for May. Concurrently, weak demand has led regional producers to lower their offer levels, anticipating increased availability in May. Negotiations for May contract prices are ongoing between European PVC producers and buyers. Some producers have initiated discussions by proposing rollovers to buyers, which have largely been rejected, while others are focusing on price reductions in light of the declining feedstock ethylene prices and the need to enhance margins, which are currently deemed unsustainable. Market participants noted that significant progress was made in April to bolster producer margins. Consumers highlighted a notable drop in the ethylene MCP for May, weak structural demand for end products, and competitive pricing from Asia during negotiations. Additionally, some consumers are adopting a wait-and-see approach regarding volume purchases, expecting more favorable offers in May. Demand for s-PVC in the European construction sector, a crucial market, remains subdued. Although there has been a slight easing of contraction month-on-month, the overall outlook remains bleak. Despite early indications of a potential easing in the sector's downturn, construction firms in the euro area continue to express pessimism about the future activity outlook. Supply and demand trends exhibited a mixed pattern, characterized by limited domestic availability due to production halts in the first and second quarters. However, global PVC supply has been increasing, and the pressure from imports is becoming apparent. European spot prices are varied, with Asian offers significantly lower than those of domestic producers, leading to a growing focus on imports from Asia. There is a healthy supply of PVC available for export from China to Europe, despite tariff uncertainties and diminished export opportunities to India. Supply disruptions continue, particularly as a regional producer remains under force majeure for S-PVC at their Tavaux facility in France, while another regional producer is still experiencing shutdowns in PVC production at their Wloclawek site in Poland. In Turkey, demand remains weak due to ongoing challenging macroeconomic and political conditions. Competitive offers are increasingly surfacing from Egypt, South Korea, and the United States, while European producers show limited willingness to match prices from other regions. Global markets have faced disruptions due to new US tariffs and Chinese counter-tariffs on US exports, prompting Asian producers to increasingly target Europe and the Middle East. Although the US arbitrage remains closed due to antidumping duties implemented in mid-2024, the rising availability is starting to impact mutual export markets such as Turkey. Additionally, E-PVC demand is also weak, particularly due to a sluggish automotive sector in Europe, leading to indications of softer contract delta pricing for e-PVC in response to this weak demand. PET: This week, PET spot prices decreased, while contract prices remained constant in the European region. European spot prices for polyethylene terephthalate continue to fall, despite a slight uptick in demand observed over the past week; however, overall demand remains weak. Market participants noted a modest rise in purchasing interest for PET, influenced by consumer apprehensions regarding potential price increases later in the month due to escalating feedstock costs in Asia. In recent weeks, PET buyers have taken a cautious stance, anticipating further price declines. Nonetheless, with US-China trade negotiations occurring in the week and tight supply conditions for paraxylene and PTA in Asia, prices have seen an uptick. As a result, offers from Asia have already increased. To sell larger volumes, European producers have sought to remain competitive, yet they reportedly struggle to match the pricing levels of their Asian counterparts. Participants in the market have indicated that while the European market is influenced by trends in Asia, the effects are not immediate, leading to a time lag. Although prices should theoretically rise, the current situation does not reflect this due to low demand in Europe. Demand had already diminished in April, and May is anticipated to be even weaker, as customers had accumulated substantial stock when they recognized that prices were no longer increasing, prompting them to cease purchases and use their current inventory. USHDPE: This week, HDPE export prices witnessed a rise in the US. US spot export prices for high-density polyethylene (HDPE) remained stable or experienced slight increases across most grades during the week ended, as market sentiment began to shift towards the perception that prices may have reached a minimum threshold. Producers maintained their current pricing levels and resisted further reductions, prompting market participants to adjust their margins to stay competitive. Additionally, cautious optimism was bolstered by the announcement of a temporary reduction in trade tensions between the US and China. The two nations agreed to a 90-day tariff reduction starting May 14, with the US lowering tariffs on most Chinese goods from 145% to 30%, while China reduced tariffs on US goods from 125% to 10%. This development raised expectations for a gradual normalization of trade flows, with China once again viewed as a viable market for US-origin materials. Market feedback indicated that previously available low-priced offers have mostly been absorbed, further supporting the idea that prices may stabilize or begin to rise. However, a level of uncertainty remained, as the tariff relief is temporary and contingent upon the results of broader trade negotiations. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices quoted higher in the US. The price of US spot export low-density polyethylene (LDPE) increased due to a rise in demand from Latin America during the week concluded. Market feedback indicated that Latin America was the most active region, with restocking activity driving increased interest in US-origin material, despite the fact that overall global demand remained relatively muted. The temporary reduction of US-China tariffs, which increased hopes for better trade flows and a possible recovery in demand, helped to boost sentiment in addition to regional support. Some market players pointed out that if trade normalization results in more robust export inquiries in the upcoming weeks, the current pricing might be considered as the market bottom. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices remained steady in the US. As ongoing supply chain disruptions continued to impact market activity, US spot export linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE) prices remained stable to slightly lower across the majority of grades in the week ended. Logistical bottlenecks, such as equipment shortages and shipping delays, have hindered the flow of materials to important international markets, maintaining pressure on export demand. Some market participants pointed out that despite these difficulties, prices might have reached the bottom, especially since competitively priced cargoes have been mostly absorbed. Though buyers continued to exercise caution due to the uncertain outlook and ongoing operational constraints that affect timely deliveries, sentiment remained mixed. PP: This week, PP export prices were assessed flat in the US. US spot export polypropylene (PP) prices remained stable in the week ended as the market adjusted to the effects of the recently announced tariff relief between the United States and China. The agreement, effective May 14, will reduce US duties on the majority of Chinese imports from 145% to 30%, while China will lower tariffs on US goods from 125% to 10%. This development was positively received by the PP market, with participants expressing cautious optimism that the temporary reduction in tariffs would facilitate trade flows and enhance demand. Reports indicated that Chinese polypropylene producers have implemented a series of price increases in anticipation of heightened downstream activity, particularly as exports of finished goods from China to the US are projected to rise under the new tariff structure. Although overall demand is still in a recovery phase, the reduction of trade barriers has instilled a sense of optimism in market sentiment, with many participants closely monitoring for indications of increased purchasing activity in the upcoming weeks. PS: This week, PS export prices marched higher in the US. Prices for both export general-purpose polystyrene (GPPS) and high-impact polystyrene (HIPS) experienced an increase during the week, driven by a recovery in upstream costs. Market players observed that the overall polystyrene sector seems to be progressing towards a more balanced condition, with supply and demand beginning to converge more closely. Although domestic PS prices remained unchanged for May, various sources indicated that the recent drop in benzene feedstock costs is likely to apply downward pressure on PS pricing in the future. Nevertheless, the current rise in export values suggests that upstream volatility is exerting a more significant short-term impact on pricing dynamics, particularly in global markets. PVC: This week, PVC export prices were assessed steady in the US. The US PVC export market remained in a wait-and-see attitude during the week-end due to low trading activity, which helped to keep prices stable overall. A possible price recovery in the upcoming weeks, fuelled by a reduction in international trade tensions, was the subject of cautious optimism from certain market participants. A significant announcement that improved market sentiment was the US and China's agreement to roll back recently imposed tariffs for at least 90 days in order to make space for more extensive trade talks. A change in pricing strategy was indicated by reports that producers and traders were withdrawing offers as expectations of a recovery grew. This changing tone is occurring in the context of limited supply from multiple producers and rising demand for PVC of US origin in a few export markets. All of these elements have combined to gradually but noticeably boost market optimism. PET: This week, PET prices were left unchanged in the US. The seasonal demand for polyethylene terephthalate (PET) spot imports in the United States has not yet gained traction in the week ending [insert date], despite a recent reduction in trade tensions between the United States and China. Generally, PET consumption increases during the summer months due to heightened demand from the beverage industry during the peak bottling season. However, market participants have observed that this anticipated seasonal increase has not yet occurred. Meanwhile, the United States and China reached an agreement to implement mutual tariff reductions during a 90-day cessation of trade hostilities, which is scheduled to commence on May 14, as announced by the Chinese government on May 12. Although the reduction in tariffs has enhanced market sentiment and may stimulate interest in imported PET, purchasing activity has remained low for the time being, as participants await more definitive indications of demand recovery.

data.title: Israeli strikes kill at least 58 Palestinians overnight
data.description:
Israel's airforce killed at least 58 Palestinians in new attacks on Gaza overnight, local health authorities said on Saturday, as the country appeared set to press ahead with a new ground offensive.

More than 300 Gazans have been killed in Israeli strikes since Thursday, according to local health authorities, one of the deadliest phases of bombardment since a truce collapsed in March. The latest strikes came after U.S. President Donald Trump ended his Middle East tour on Friday with no apparent progress towards a new ceasefire.

"Since midnight, we have received 58 martyrs, while a large number of victims remain under the rubble. The situation inside the hospital is catastrophic," said the director of the Indonesian Hospital in northern Gaza, Marwan Al-Sultan.

Israel's military said on Saturday it is conducting extensive strikes and mobilizing troops as part of preparations to expand operations in the Gaza Strip and achieve "operational control" in areas of the Palestinian enclave.

The escalation, which includes the build-up of armoured forces along the border, is part of the initial stages of "Operation Gideon's Wagons", which Israel says is aimed at defeating Hamas and getting its hostages back.

An Israeli defence official said earlier this month that the operation would not be launched before Trump concluded his visit to the Middle East.

United Nations experts warn that famine looms in Gaza after Israel blocked aid deliveries to the strip 76 days ago, with UN aid chief Tom Fletcher asking the Security Council this week if it would act to "prevent genocide".

Trump on Friday acknowledged Gaza's growing hunger crisis and the need for aid deliveries, as international pressure grows on Israel to resume ceasefire talks and end its blockade of Gaza.

Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on May 5 that Israel was planning an expanded, intensive offensive against Hamas as his security cabinet approved plans that could involve seizing the entire Gaza Strip and controlling aid.

Israel's declared goal in Gaza is the elimination of the military and governmental capabilities of Hamas, which attacked Israeli communities on October 7, 2023, killing around 1,200 people and seizing about 250 hostages.

Its military campaign has devastated the tiny, crowded enclave, pushing nearly all inhabitants from their homes and killing more than 53,000 people, according to Gaza health authorities.

data.title: Lammy says UK, US working to ensure enduring India-Pakistan ceasefire, dialogue
data.description:
Key points:
  • Lammy says countries should respect treaties after India suspends water pact
  • India-Pakistan ceasefire is fragile, say diplomats, analysts
  • UK's Lammy accuses Russia of 'obfuscation' over Ukraine

Britain is working with the U.S. to ensure a ceasefire between India and Pakistan endures and that "confidence-building measures" and dialogue take place, foreign minister David Lammy said on Saturday.

Pakistan has said Britain and other countries, in addition to the United States, played a major role in de-escalating the worst fighting in decades between the nuclear-armed South Asian rivals, which erupted last week. A rapid diplomatic effort to broker the ceasefire succeeded on May 10, but diplomats and analysts say it remains fragile.

"We will continue to work with the United States to ensure that we get an enduring ceasefire, to ensure that dialogue is happening and to work through with Pakistan and India how we can get to confidence and confidence-building measures between the two sides," Lammy told Reuters in Pakistan's capital Islamabad at the end of a two-day visit.

Pakistan and India fired missiles onto each other's territory during weeks of tensions after a deadly attack on tourists in the contested region of Kashmir that New Delhi blames on Islamabad. Pakistan denies involvement.

U.S. President Donald Trump said after the ceasefire was struck that talks should take place in a third-country venue but no dates or location for the talks have been announced.

"These are two neighbours with a long history but they are two neighbours that have barely been able to speak to one other over this past period, and we want to ensure that we do not see further escalation and that the ceasefire endures," Lammy said.

Asked about India's suspension of the Indus Water Treaty, potentially squeezing Pakistan's water supply, Lammy said: "We would urge all sides to meet their treaty obligations."

Delhi said last month it had "put in abeyance" its participation in the 1960 pact, which governs use of the Indus river system, a move Pakistan says it would consider an act of war if it disrupted access to water in the agriculturally dependent nation.

Lammy said Britain would also continue to work with Pakistan on countering "terrorism", saying that it is "a terrible blight on this country and its people, and of course on the region."

'OBFUSCATION' BY RUSSIA ON UKRAINE

Lammy accused Moscow of obfuscating after talks between Ukraine and Russia on a possible ceasefire ended in less than two hours and Trump said "nothing could happen" until he had met directly with Russian President Vladimir Putin.

"Yet again we are seeing obfuscation on the Russian side and unwillingness to get serious about the enduring peace that is now required in Ukraine," Lammy said. "Once again Russia is not serious."

"At what point do we say to Putin enough is enough?" he said.

data.title: Ukrainian drones hit ammunition depot in Russia-occupied Crimea, says security service source
data.description:
Ukrainian drones have hit an ammunition depot in Russia-occupied Crimea, causing a powerful detonation at warehouses where military equipment, weapons and fuel were stored, a source in Ukraine's security service told Reuters on Saturday.

The source said the depot belonged to the 126th brigade of Russia's Coastal Defence and was located in the village of Perevalne in the southern part of the Black Sea peninsula. Russia seized Crimea from Ukraine in 2014.

Reuters could not independently confirm the report.

data.title: British police charge three Iranians in counter terrorism probe
data.description:
British police have charged three Iranian men with offences under the National Security Act after a major counter-terrorism investigation, the police said on Saturday.

British counter-terrorism police arrested eight men including seven Iranians earlier this month in two separate operations in what the British interior minister called some of the biggest investigations of their kind in recent years.

Mostafa Sepahvand, Farhad Javadi Manesh, and Shapoor Qalehali Khani Noori were charged with engaging in conduct likely to assist a foreign intelligence service between August 14, 2024, and February 16, 2025, the police said in a statement.

The foreign state to which the charges relate is Iran, they added.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi has previously said he was "disturbed" to learn that Iranian citizens had been arrested by British authorities.

The British government has placed Iran on the highest tier of its foreign influence register, requiring Tehran to register everything it does to exert political influence in the UK.

data.title: Iran's President Pezeshkian says Trump speaks of peace and threatens at same time
data.description:
Iran’s President Masoud Pezeshkian said on Saturday that U.S. President Donald Trump speaks simultaneously about peace and threats.

"Which should we believe?" Pezeshkian said at a naval event in Tehran. "On the one hand, he speaks of peace and on the other, he threatens with the most advanced tools of mass killing."

Tehran would continue Iran-U.S. nuclear talks but is not afraid of threats. "We are not seeking war," he said.

Trump said on Friday that Iran had a U.S. proposal about its nuclear programme and knows it needs to move quickly to resolve a decades-long dispute.

"More importantly, they know they have to move quickly or something bad — something bad's going to happen," Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One after departing the United Arab Emirates on Friday, according to an audio recording of the remarks.

However, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi in a post on X said Tehran had not received a U.S. proposal. "There is no scenario in which Iran abandons its hard-earned right to (uranium) enrichment for peaceful purposes..." he said.

Pezeshkian said Iran would not "back down from our legitimate rights".

"Because we refuse to bow to bullying, they say we are source of instability in the region," he said.

A fourth round of Iran-U.S. talks ended in Oman last Sunday. A new round has not been scheduled yet.

data.title: Ethereum Price Risks Further Crash To $2,350 With Lower Lows Formation
data.description:
The Ethereum price has ranged low now after making a new monthly high back on Tuesday. This increase had come as a much-needed relief for the crypto market, which had watched the ETH price struggled while Bitcoin thrived. However, the bullish breakout has not lasted long as bears have once again taken control and sellers are now dominating. Given this recent trend, it is possible that the Ethereum price has seen the end of price decline.

Ethereum Lower Lows Present Troubling Trend

Crypto analyst Gianni Pichichero has explained what could be going on with the Ethereum price and why the current trend could be worrying. This goes through the different processes and how the altcoin has been moving since the start of the week, starting from Monday’s bullish rise to the bearish reversal that took the market by surprise.

Gianni explained that the opening range for the week had established the current monthly high before breaking low. This showed an entry of large players into the market as the Ethereum price was pushed up rapidly to touch the $2,700 mark for the first time in over a month. This had set a bullish tone for the week, following into the next day as Tuesday also showed recovery strength,

The next day, Tuesday, the Ethereum price did pump once again and placed a higher high than Monday, suggesting that a continuation was in play. The day also closed out in the green as ETH bulls remained dominant through the trading day.

By Wednesday, there had been a turn in the market, whereas the previous days were dominated by bullish rallies, consolidation was the order of the day. This brought the Ethereum price back inside the opening range high of the week and then marked the first red close of the week.

This first red close, Gianni explains, was a bearish signal. It initially didn’t signal that the Ethereum price would continue to crash. However, it did show that the bullishness that began on Monday might finally be over. Then, by Thursday, it was already a full-blown reversal as the market tested the previous day’s lows. Thursday’s red close was just as bearish as the market turned in expectation of bearish news.

Related Reading: Is Bitcoin Price Turning Bullish Or Bearish? Crypto Analyst Reveals Critical Levels To Watch

The formation of lower lows on both days has been worrying, and the analyst outlined in the chart that there could be a possible breakdown of the price. In this case, the Ethereum price could again crash back below $2,400, wiping out a notable amount of gains accumulated over the last few weeks.

As this unfolds, Gianni suggests that there would not be any crazy moves, but that focus should be on looking for opportunities to sell high in the current market. “ I will be looking for any sell high opportunity after the news, reinforced with bearish price action, as triple tops, double tops and any kind of reversal patterns into the most recent high in place,” he said.

data.title: New Zealand man arrested in $265M crypto scam tied to FBI probe
data.description:
A man from Wellington, the capital city of New Zealand, has been arrested in connection with an FBI-led investigation into a global cryptocurrency fraud operation that allegedly stole $450 million New Zealand dollars ($265 million).

According to New Zealand Police, the man is one of 13 individuals charged after authorities executed search warrants across Auckland, Wellington, and California over the past three days.

The charges stem from allegations that members of an organized criminal group manipulated seven victims to obtain large amounts of cryptocurrency, which was then laundered through multiple platforms between March and August 2024.

The US Department of Justice has indicted the man under federal law, including charges of racketeering, conspiracy to commit wire fraud, and conspiracy to commit money laundering, per the announcement.

Scammer used stolen funds to purchase luxury vehicles

Prosecutors allege the stolen funds were used to purchase $9 million worth of luxury vehicles and spent lavishly on high-end goods, including designer handbags, watches, and clothing, as well as services such as nightclub access, private security, and rentals in Los Angeles, Miami, and the Hamptons.

The accused appeared in Auckland District Court and was granted bail with interim name suppression. He is scheduled to reappear on July 3.

“We have worked closely with our law enforcement colleagues in the United States in support of their investigation,” the police stated. They added:

“Today’s search warrant and arrest reflects the importance of international partnerships where criminals are operating across borders.”

The investigation remains ongoing.

Crypto thefts surge to $360M in April

Digital asset thefts skyrocketed in April 2025, with nearly $360 million stolen across 18 separate hacking incidents, according to data from blockchain security firm PeckShield.

The figure marks a staggering 990% jump from March when reported losses stood at just $33 million. The sharp rise was largely attributed to a single unauthorized Bitcoin transfer that accounted for the bulk of the month’s losses.

On April 28, blockchain analyst ZachXBT identified a suspicious $330 million BTC transaction. The incident was later confirmed as a social engineering attack that targeted an elderly US resident, resulting in one of the largest individual crypto thefts to date.

data.title: I Tried Living in the Suburbs Without a Car. It Was Harder Than I Thought. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Neal Templin

I put a lot of thought into living without a car after my marriage ended.

I rented a one-bedroom apartment in New Jersey near a grocery store, restaurants, and the post office. I'm a three-minute walk from a train line that goes into New York City and a 15-minute walk from my gym. My digs are across the street from a gorgeous park with a gurgling brook.

Talk about a perfect situation for someone without a car.

That feeling didn't even last a year. Three months ago, I bought a car. Blame dating, doctors, and shopping. All three are a hassle without a set of wheels.

My experience illustrates the challenges society faces in reducing its dependence on automobiles. Proponents of New Urbanism talk about building walkable cities that mix retail and residential with reliable transport so there's less reason to hop into a car. My little New Jersey commuter town has all of that in spades. If I were forced to live without a car, I could have. Still, I opted for four wheels.

I don't have a good excuse for owning a car, either. Parents, for example, drive kids to swim lessons, friends' houses, and late-night visits to the emergency room with a suddenly worrisome cough. I'm close to retirement age, and my children are long grown up. My best friends in New Jersey live within walking distance.

I started with the best of intentions. I wanted to live a low-carbon life. I believe global warming will be a growing problem over the coming decades, and I have a grandchild who will be there to suffer through it. Plus, I have always been a walker. With my two feet and judicious use of Uber, I figured I didn't need a car.

It seemed liberating at first. I didn't need to rent a parking space, and didn't need to pay for car insurance or maintenance. Who cares if I have to spend a few hundred dollars a month on ride services? It's still far cheaper than owning an automobile and would reduce my costs later in retirement.

I thought I had done something admirable by helping the planet and saving myself money at the same time. Look at me. In fact, I thought, why can't everyone be like me? Yep, I admit it — I was proud about living without a car. The ancient Greeks coined the word hubris, and it never ended well in their myths.

The problem started, oddly enough, with a car. My ex-wife and I had an amicable split. She has spent chunks of the past year in our native state of California, where her family lives. Whenever she hopped a flight to the Golden State, she left her car with me.

So I got to compare living without a car to living with a car. And the car made life far easier than I had anticipated.

When I didn't have a car, I had to prepare for everything. The local library was closed for a year, and I had to use the one in a neighboring town, which was a 40-minute walk. Getting there, finding books, and walking back consumed a couple of hours.

I picked a podiatrist who was a mile and a half away. Like the library, each podiatrist visit consumed a couple of hours.

Of course, I could have taken Uber or Lyft. But it seemed wrong to spend $15 to go somewhere I could walk in half an hour. Then it's another $15 for a ride home. So I didn't use ride services except when the distances were more than a couple of miles. That turned out to be a mistake.

Shopping was another issue. The little market within walking distance doesn't have everything I need. A friend who regularly drove to a bigger market farther away offered to take me along, but I felt guilty asking for a lift. I ended up buying huge quantities of staples at the bigger market whenever I had my ex's vehicle.

The worst part about not having a car was dating. Yes, you can Uber to a bar or restaurant. But I dated a woman who often wanted me to pick her up at her house. And she was never, ever ready on time. (She told me for the article it takes time to look as good as she does.) What then? I suppose I could have taken an Uber to her house, waited, then called another Uber to the restaurant, and then an Uber to take her home, and another Uber back to my place. It's a lot of Uber.

Instead, I asked her to meet me at a restaurant whenever I didn't have a car. It wasn't the best way to impress a gal.

By Christmas last year, I knew I needed my own wheels. So just before my ex returned to New Jersey after spending the holidays in California and reclaimed her car, I crossed the vehicular Rubicon. I shelled out $34,000 for a new Toyota Camry hybrid that gets more than 40 miles to the gallon.

I love my new car. Every time I get in it, I feel privileged and pampered.

The bad news is that I'm paying dearly for this feeling.

Edmunds has a calculator that shows the total costs of car ownership, including depreciation, fuel, maintenance, insurance, taxes, and financing costs. My basic Camry ends up costing more than $41,000 over five years.

I won't sugarcoat things. If you look at the true cost of car ownership, I almost surely would have saved considerable money by using ride services instead. But psychologically, I couldn't justify spending $30 for transportation to a supermarket to buy $3 crackers. In short, I was done in by being too much of a cheapskate to use ride services more often.

Living months without a car did change my driving habits for the better. I still walk to the gym instead of driving because I now view it as part of the workout. If I need to buy one or two things, I still walk to the nearby grocery store. If I need a bunch of things, I hop in the car and buy them at the big supermarket a few miles away. And what a luxury that is!

I work from home most days in my current job. And when I do go into Barron's office in New York City, I take the train.

I'm only driving a little more than 400 miles a month — less than half what the average American drives — and consuming around 10 gallons of gasoline a month.

And make no mistake: I'm driving those 400 miles with a big smile on my face.

Write to Neal Templin at neal.templin@barrons.com

data.title: Arcadis' shareholders re-appoint Supervisory Board members and approve dividend
data.description:
rcadis 2025 Annual General Meeting

Arcadis' shareholders re-appoint Supervisory Board members and approve dividend

  • Shareholders re-appointed Michiel Lap and Carla Mahieu to the Supervisory Board
  • Shareholders approved the company's dividend proposal of €1.00 per ordinary share

Amsterdam, 17 May 2025 – Arcadis , the world’s leading company in delivering data-driven sustainable design, engineering, and consultancy solutions for natural and built assets, confirms that all resolutions that were brought up for vote were adopted during its annual General Meeting earlier today.

Michiel Lap was reappointed to the Supervisory of Arcadis N.V. for a final term of two years. Mr. Lap has been a member of the Supervisory Board since 2015, and Chair of the Supervisory Board since 2022. Mr. Lap will remain Chair of the Supervisory Board, Chair of the Selection Committee, member of the Audit and Risk Committee and member of the Remuneration Committee.

In addition, Carla Mahieu was reappointed to the Supervisory Board for a second four-year term. Ms. Mahieu has been a member of the Supervisory Board and Chair of the Remuneration Committee since 2021. Ms. Mahieu will remain Chair of the Remuneration Committee and member of the Selection Committee.

Shareholders approved the company's dividend proposal of €1.00 per ordinary share, representing 33% of net income from operations in 2024. The proposal is in accordance with Arcadis’ dividend policy which aims for a pay-out of 30-40% of net income from operations.

The voting outcome per agenda item will be published on the Arcadis website in the coming days.

ARCADIS INVESTOR RELATIONS

Christine Disch | +31 (0)615376020 | christine.disch@arcadis.com

ABOUT ARCADIS

Arcadis is the world’s leading company delivering data-driven sustainable design, engineering, and consultancy solutions for natural and built assets. We are more than 35,000 architects, data analysts, designers, engineers, project planners, water management and sustainability experts, all driven by our passion for improving quality of life. As part of our commitment to accelerating a planet positive future, we work with our clients to make sustainable project choices, combining digital and human innovation, and embracing future-focused skills across the environment, energy and water, buildings, transport, and infrastructure sectors. We operate in over 30 countries, and reported €5.0 billion in gross revenues for 2024. www.arcadis.com

data.title: Putin to host first Russia-Arab summit in October, Russian agencies report
data.description:
Russian President Vladimir Putin has invited all leaders and the secretary general of the Arab League for the first Russia-Arab summit on October 15, Russia's news agencies reported on Saturday, citing a statement from the Kremlin.

"I am confident that this meeting will contribute to the further strengthening of mutually beneficial, multifaceted cooperation between our countries and will help in finding ways to ensure peace, security, and stability in the Middle East and North Africa," Interfax agency cited Putin as saying in the statement.

The Arab League, a regional organisation of Arab states in the Middle East and parts of Africa, has 22 member states who have pledged, among others, to cooperate on political, economic and military affairs in the region.

The reports came following a four-day trip by U.S. President Donald Trump through the Gulf region this week, during which Washington said it had secured several deals, including a $600 billion commitment by Saudi Arabia to invest in the U.S., $142 billion in arms sales to the kingdom, and an AI partnership with the United Arab Emirates.
